title: Minsky & Papert's Critique
concept: In 1969, Marvin Minsky and Seymour Papert's book 'Perceptrons' mathematically proved the linear-separability limitation, and their rigorous critique caused researchers and funders to lose confidence in neural networks.

exercises.prompt: Recap: Why can't a single perceptron learn XOR?

options.text: XOR isn't linearly separable — no single straight line divides its true and false points
Perceptrons don't understand the number zero

exercises.explain: Right — it's a geometric wall, not a lack of training.

cards.title: A book that stopped a field
cards.body: In 1969, two MIT professors published a book. It wasn't science fiction, and it wasn't a hype piece — it was dense math. But that one book did something few books ever do: it slowed down an entire scientific field for over a decade.

cards.title: The book: Perceptrons (1969)
cards.body: Marvin Minsky and Seymour Papert, both AI researchers at MIT, published Perceptrons in 1969. Using rigorous mathematical proofs, they showed exactly which patterns a single-layer perceptron could and couldn't learn — and XOR was their star example of "can't."

cards.title: A judge's ruling, not a rumor
cards.body: Earlier critics had grumbled that perceptrons seemed limited, but it was just talk. Minsky and Papert's book was more like a judge's official ruling backed by hard evidence — a rigorous proof, not a hunch. That's what made it so hard to argue with, and so damaging to perceptron research.

cards.title: What they actually proved
cards.body: Minsky and Papert didn't just point at XOR and shrug. They proved, with math, that ANY pattern which isn't linearly separable — XOR included — is permanently out of reach for a single perceptron, no matter how it's trained or how long you wait.

cards.title: They didn't kill neural networks
cards.body: A popular myth is that Minsky and Papert claimed ALL neural networks were useless forever. They didn't — their proof was specifically about single-layer perceptrons. Multi-layer networks weren't ruled out, but at the time nobody had a good way to train them yet, so the door looked shut.

cards.title: Before vs. after the book
cards.body: The mood shift in the AI research world:

left.text:
Rosenblatt's perceptron sparks excitement
Big promises, growing funding

right.text:
Rigorous proof of limits published
Confidence and funding dry up

exercises.prompt: What did Minsky and Papert's 1969 book 'Perceptrons' mathematically prove?

options.text: Single-layer perceptrons cannot learn patterns that aren't linearly separable, like XOR
Computers would never be as smart as humans
XOR gates don't exist in real circuits

exercises.explain: Their proof was specific and rigorous: it targeted the mathematical limits of single-layer perceptrons.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Minsky and Papert proved that ALL neural networks, including ones with multiple layers, could never solve XOR.
exercises.explain: False — their proof applied to SINGLE-layer perceptrons. Multi-layer networks weren't proven impossible, just untrainable with the tools available at the time.

exercises.sentence: In 1969, ___ and Seymour Papert published the book 'Perceptrons.'
exercises.explain: Marvin Minsky co-authored the book with Seymour Papert, rigorously proving the perceptron's limits.

exercises.prompt: Which of these was the CENTRAL example Minsky and Papert used to demonstrate a perceptron's limits?
exercises.explain: XOR became the textbook example of a pattern no single perceptron could ever separate.

exercises.prompt: A student argues: 'Minsky and Papert just had an opinion that perceptrons were bad — other researchers could've just disagreed and kept funding them.' What's the flaw in this argument?

options.text: Their claim was a mathematical proof, not an opinion — you can't out-argue a proof, only find a new approach that gets around its exact conditions
There's no flaw — perceptrons really were just badly designed from the start
Minsky and Papert were not real scientists
The book was never actually published

exercises.explain: Exactly. A rigorous proof is different from a hot take — it doesn't get overturned by debate, only by changing the conditions (like using more than one layer). That's precisely why the shockwaves it sent through AI funding were so hard to reverse. Next: what happened to the field after the shockwaves hit.
